title: Change Batching
use_for: aggregating local changes before pipeline reruns
coalescing bursts of updates into bounded batch windows
reducing overhead from many tiny incremental reruns
shaping change volume before invalidation and execution
deciding when to merge, queue, or flush change sets
do_not_use_for: basic source-versus-derived modeling without bursty change input
generic scheduler queues detached from pipeline products
rules: batch changes only when the batch window costs less than many tiny reruns
make merge and flush rules explicit
preserve source truth while coalescing derived update work
define when batching falls back to broad rerun
keep batch ownership and backlog visible
procedure: Define the incoming change kinds and rates.
Choose whether changes queue, merge, or flush immediately.
Define the batch window and flush triggers.
Map batch output to invalidation and stage rerun paths.
Measure whether batching reduces overhead without violating latency goals.

primary_when: the request is specifically about batching, coalescing, or flushing pipeline changes before rerun
the request is about handling bursts of local updates without paying many tiny rerun costs
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about stage decomposition with no change burst problem
the request is about generic queueing unrelated to pipeline recompute

solves: how to aggregate bursts of small changes before driving invalidation and rerun
how to reduce overhead from too-frequent incremental updates
how to choose merge, queue, and flush policy for change sets
does_not_solve: source-domain semantics outside the change stream

core_principles: A good batch window reduces overhead without hiding unacceptable latency.
Change batching should shape derived update work, not rewrite source truth semantics.
Merge and flush rules must be explicit so backlog and correctness remain reviewable.
constraints: Batching must not introduce more latency than the owning runtime phase can tolerate.
Coalescing rules must preserve correctness for affected derived products.
Backlog growth and flush behavior must remain measurable.
decision_rules: Use change-batching as the owner when the main problem is too many tiny change-driven reruns rather than invalidation semantics alone.
If the batch window can violate responsiveness, attach work-budgeting before adopting larger batches.
If changes supersede one another naturally, prefer merge semantics over unbounded queue growth.
If structural changes accumulate, flush to a deliberate broad rerun instead of forcing them through fine-grained coalescing.

preferred_patterns: explicit change queues or buffers with merge semantics
flush triggers tied to phase boundaries or size thresholds
batch outputs feeding retained-product invalidation cleanly
bounded backlog and measurable batch latency
avoid_patterns: unbounded change queues with no merge or flush discipline
batch windows chosen by intuition alone
coalescing that mutates derived state directly with no clear change-set output
fine-grained reruns surviving even after batching would obviously be cheaper

recipes.name: Coalesce bursty pipeline updates
recipes.when: Use when local updates arrive faster than the pipeline should rerun them one by one.
steps: Define which changes may merge and which must remain ordered.
Choose the flush trigger: time window, size threshold, or lifecycle phase.
Emit one batched change set into incremental recompute.
Measure rerun overhead, latency, and backlog growth under burst load.

pitfalls: Batching changes without defining acceptable latency.
Allowing backlog to grow because merge and flush rules are vague.
Confusing source-truth changes with the derived work representation being batched.
review_checklist: Are merge and flush rules explicit?
Is the batch window compatible with responsiveness requirements?
Can backlog and batch latency be measured?
Does batching feed invalidation and rerun cleanly instead of bypassing them?

references.title: Local Reference: Change Batching
references.interpretation: Use this first for merge rules, flush policy, and batching change streams before incremental rerun.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for practical change batching.

references.title: Local Reference: Incremental Recompute
references.interpretation: Use this to keep batching aligned with retained products, partial reruns, and fallback to full recompute.
references.why: Use to connect batching policy with the existing incremental update model.
